title: Identifying Dialectics For each group, check the most dialectical response. • •

From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission

Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 16c
Identifying Dialectics
For each group, check the most dialectical response.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 1A.	Pay attention to your effect on others.

 1B.	Assume that others’ reactions to you
are unrelated to your treatment of
them.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 5A.	Examine a difficult relationship by
looking at how the interactions over
time between you and the other
person may be problematic.

 5B.	Assume that difficulties in a
relationship are caused completely by
you or by the other person.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Saying:

 2A.	“I know I am right about this.”

 2B.	“I can see your point of view, even
though I do not agree with it.”

 2C.	“The way you are thinking doesn’t
make any sense.”
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Saying:

 6A.	“It is hopeless. I cannot do it.”

 6B.	“This is a breeze. I’ve got no
problems.”

 6C.	“This is really hard for me, and I am
going to keep trying.”
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Saying:

 3A.	“Everyone always treats me unfairly.”

 3B.	“I believe the coach should reconsider
his decision to cut me from the team.”

 3C.	“Coaches know best who to keep on
teams and who to cut.”
••••••••

 7A.	When you disagree with someone,
be sure and be very clear about your
point of view.

 7B.	When you disagree with someone, try
and see their point of view.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 4A.	Judge friends as disloyal and uncaring
if they start changing in ways you don’t
like.

 4B.	Accept that interests change.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 8A.	Demand that relationships be stable
without changing.

 8B.	Embrace change and see it as
inevitable.
Note. Adapted in part from Miller, A. L., Rathus, J. H., & Linehan, M. M. (2007). Dialectical behavior therapy with suicidal adolescents. New York:
Guilford Press. Copyright 2007 by The Guilford Press. Adapted by permission.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Identifying Dialectics For each group, check the most dialectical response. • •
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
